serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Moo Ping is 3 skewers, weighing approximately 150 g, typically consumed as a street food snack or light meal alongside sticky rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, shoulder, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 168329)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai moo ping exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central grilled pork shoulder data (FDC ID 168329). Marinade contributions (fish sauce, palm sugar, soy sauce, coconut milk, oyster sauce) account for the elevated sodium (533 mg/100g), sugar (2.67 g/100g), and saturated fat (2.67 g/100g) relative to plain grilled pork. Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the adapted USDA source profile and have been retained. HPB Singapore database was consulted as a secondary cross-reference but does not contain a direct moo ping entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai moo ping in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed source. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous profile of grilled lean pork with a palm-sugar-based marinade. Pork itself has negligible GI; the small carbohydrate contribution (4 g/100g) comes primarily from palm sugar and soy sauce in the marinade. Palm sugar has a GI of approximately 35–54 (low range) per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). The blended dish GI is estimated at 45 (low). GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 4.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.7 g, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 533 mg/100g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Moo Ping to occasional consumption and request reduced marinade (fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce) to meaningfully lower the sodium load per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is only 4.0 g/100g with a low estimated GI of 45; high protein (20 g/100g) moderates gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Moo Ping is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without sticky rice; the low carbohydrate content and high protein make it a blood-glucose-friendly protein source, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is elevated at 166.67 mg/100g and sodium is high at 533 mg/100g; saturated fat is moderate at 2.67 g/100g from pork fat and coconut milk in the marinade.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Moo Ping in moderation, limit portion size to 1–2 skewers, and request a lighter marinade to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake; the high protein content is beneficial but the cholesterol and sodium levels warrant caution.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium at 533 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold associated with worsening RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Moo Ping due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork and the high sodium from fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce in the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Moo Ping (หมูปิ้ง) is a classic Thai street food consisting of grilled marinated pork skewers, widely sold by street vendors throughout Thailand and popular in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The dish is made from thinly sliced or minced pork shoulder marinated in a mixture of fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, palm sugar, coconut milk, garlic, coriander root, and white pepper, then threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal until caramelised and slightly charred. The result is a savoury, mildly sweet, smoky, and tender pork skewer with a glossy, caramelised exterior. Moo Ping is typically served as a snack or light meal, often accompanied by sticky rice (khao niao). Nutritionally, a 150 g serving (3 skewers) provides approximately 280 kcal, 30 g protein, 15 g fat, and only 6 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is notably high at 533 mg/100g due to the multi-sauce marinade, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 45), and the glycemic load per serving is very low at 2.7 g, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without sticky rice. Cholesterol is elevated at approximately 167 mg/100g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, fish, or shellfish allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai moo ping exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central grilled pork shoulder data (FDC ID 168329) with marinade contributions estimated. GI is an analogous estimate based on palm sugar GI range from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and marinade estimation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Thai-style marinated grilled pork is not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168329) or HPB Singapore database at the dish level as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific dish preparation in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168329, HPB Singapore database). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for fresh pork grilled without hydrogenated fats per USDA FoodData Central. 4. The input record contained inconsistencies: ethnic_tags incorrectly listed 'Seafood Dish' and 'Prawn' for a pork dish; these have been corrected. The caution referencing 'prawns' in the original health_suitability has been removed as it is factually incorrect for moo ping. 5. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; the 3-skewer (150 g) is used as the canonical serving size consistent with the product name. 6. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Thailand as moo ping is a Thai-origin dish, though it is widely sold in Singapore. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45, low) based on palm sugar GI range from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for moo ping was found in approved sources. 8. nutrient_source uses USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168329) as the sole approved primary source; HPB Singapore was consulted as a secondary cross-reference only and does not contain a direct moo ping entry.
